result.description: Xylella fastidiosa is a harmful bacterium that lives in a plant’s xylem (the tubes that move water). It clogs these tubes, so parts of the plant dry out even when the soil is moist. Typical signs include brown, crispy leaf edges (leaf scorch), yellowing between veins, stunted growth, one-sided dieback on shoots or branches, early leaf drop, and small or shrivelled fruit. It spreads mainly by small sap-feeding insects such as spittlebugs and sharpshooters, and by moving infected plants or cuttings. Many plants can be affected, including olive, grapevine, citrus, almond, cherry, lavender, rosemary, oleander, oak, and maple. This is a regulated disease in many regions.
result.treatment:
Biological: Remove and securely dispose of badly affected plants by sealing them in bags and binning them, and do not compost them. Prune out dead or declining branches in dry weather and disinfect tools between cuts with 70 percent alcohol. Water consistently to avoid drought stress and mulch to maintain soil moisture. Avoid heavy nitrogen fertiliser that promotes soft, vulnerable growth. Reduce weedy groundcovers that host spittlebug nymphs by mowing or removing them before insects mature. Use fine insect mesh on high value plants where practical. Maintain good hygiene and avoid sharing cuttings from unknown sources.

Chemical: Where permitted, reduce insect vectors by using horticultural oil or insecticidal soap targeted at nymphs on nearby weeds and groundcover. Apply kaolin clay sprays to create a protective film that discourages feeding on some crops.

Prevention: Buy plants from reputable, certified suppliers. Isolate new plants for a few weeks and watch for symptoms before placing them near established plants. Clean and disinfect pruning tools between plants. Manage weeds and tall grasses that shelter spittlebugs. Do not move plants, cuttings, or firewood from areas under restriction. Water deeply and consistently, and mulch to reduce stress. Inspect your garden regularly and contact your local plant health authority if you suspect xylella.
